worth_it_explanation: Naming is the spine of every report a publisher pulls out of SpringServe. When supply tags, demand tags, and line items carry consistent, parseable names, a report can be sliced by environment, partner, deal, or campaign without guesswork, and a stray fill or a margin problem can be traced to the object that caused it. When names drift, the same report turns into a manual reconciliation. This check is a read of the names already in the account, so it costs review time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a renaming pass the publisher can schedule rather than a structural change.
intro: Naming conventions decide whether a publisher can read their own SpringServe account at a glance. Supply tags, demand tags, and line items are the objects a report groups by, so the names attached to them carry the structure of every breakdown a publisher builds. This subcategory is about the convention itself, whether names are consistent and parseable, rather than the targeting or pricing those objects carry. These checks confirm that the naming reflects a deliberate scheme a person and a report can both follow, and that deal and campaign names stay identifiable across the account.

audit_checks.title: Supply tags, demand tags, and line items do not follow a consistent naming convention
audit_checks.impact_rationale: SpringServe reporting groups by the objects a publisher names, so when supply tags, demand tags, and line items carry inconsistent or unparseable names, a report cannot be sliced cleanly by environment, partner, or use case. The data is still there, but reading it turns into manual reconciliation, and a margin or fill problem becomes harder to trace to the object that caused it.

educate.base:
SpringServe is a video and Connected TV (CTV) ad server, and the objects a publisher manages in it, supply tags, demand tags, and line items, are the same objects SpringServe reporting groups by. A report broken out by supply tag, by demand partner, or by line item is only as readable as the names attached to those objects. When the names follow a consistent scheme, a breakdown reads at a glance and any single row points straight back to the object behind it. When the names drift, the report still runs, but reading it means remembering which cryptic label belongs to which placement.

A parseable convention encodes the facts a publisher slices by directly in the name, in a fixed order, with a fixed separator. SpringServe already records the environment for each supply tag (e.g., desktop, mobile web, in-app, or CTV) and lets a publisher attach a supply partner and labels, so the convention should line up with those structural fields rather than fight them. A name built from environment, partner, and use case in a predictable order lets a person filter a long list by typing a fragment, and lets a report group rows that share a prefix.

The cost of an inconsistent convention is not a broken account. It is friction that compounds. A few names use one separator and the rest use another. One person abbreviates a partner and the next spells it out. A test tag launched months ago still sits in production with a name that gives no hint it was ever a test. Each of these is small alone, and together they turn a routine report into a reconciliation exercise that a clean convention would have removed.

A few signs point to naming that is not consistent or parseable:
  • Supply tags, demand tags, or line items mix separators, abbreviations, or word order so similar objects do not sort or filter together
  • Names omit the facts a publisher slices reporting by, such as environment, partner, or use case, so a report cannot group on them without manual cleanup
  • Test or temporary objects carry production-style names with nothing to flag them, so they blend into live inventory in a report
You may want to confirm that a single documented convention covers supply tags, demand tags, and line items, and that the names already in the account follow it. One approach is to agree on a field order and separator, write it down, and rename the objects that diverge in a scheduled pass rather than all at once.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster running channels through SpringServe, the inventory spans live linear breaks, video-on-demand pre-rolls, and mid-roll pods, often across both an app and a CTV environment. You may want the convention to encode the environment and the placement type so a report can separate live-channel fill from on-demand fill without manual grouping, and so a CTV supply tag never gets confused with its mobile-web counterpart.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher running product videos, how-to series, or shoppable segments through SpringServe, the video inventory sits alongside editorial video and carries different value. You may want the convention to mark the commercial inventory distinctly from the editorial inventory in the supply tag and line item names, so a report can read the higher-intent placements on their own rather than blended into a single average.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for supply tags, demand tags, and line items is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the names of supply tags, demand tags, and line items in the account against a single convention, and note where names diverge or omit the facts reporting groups by.

springserve_ui.label: SpringServe UI
steps: Open the supply tag list and read the names against the environment, partner, and label fields each tag already carries, noting any tag whose name does not match its own fields or the names of comparable tags.
Open the demand tag list and check whether demand tags follow the same scheme, with a consistent separator, word order, and partner abbreviation.
Open the line item list and confirm the line item names follow the convention and can be parsed back to the inventory and demand they represent.
Flag any object whose name omits the environment, partner, or use case that a report would need to group on, and any test or temporary object whose name does not mark it as such.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by supply tag, then by demand tag, then by line item, and read the row labels as they appear.
Note any breakdown where similar objects do not sort or group together because their names diverge, which is the readability cost the convention is meant to remove.
Record which dimensions a publisher wants to slice by, such as environment, partner, or use case, that the current names cannot support without manual cleanup.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a single naming convention is documented and whether the supply tags, demand tags, and line items in the account follow it. A setup where one convention covers all three object types, names encode the facts reporting groups by, and test objects are marked distinctly passes. A setup where names diverge across objects, omit the dimensions a report needs, or let test objects blend into production is a fail recorded with the object types and the divergences found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a naming convention is documented and which objects diverge from it above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not follow a consistent, parseable naming convention across its SpringServe supply tags, demand tags, and line items, so reporting cannot be sliced cleanly by environment, partner, or use case without manual cleanup. SpringServe groups reports by these objects, so the names they carry decide how readable every breakdown is, and divergent names turn a routine report into a reconciliation exercise. {{voice_recommend}} agreeing on a single convention that encodes environment, partner, and use case in a fixed order with a fixed separator, documenting it, and renaming the objects that diverge in a scheduled pass. After the rename, rebuild a report broken out by supply tag and line item to confirm the rows group as intended.

audit_checks.title: Deal and campaign names are not consistent or identifiable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal or campaign whose name does not say who the buyer is, what the terms are, or which inventory it covers cannot be matched back to its agreement when a report or a discrepancy needs reconciling. The deal still transacts, but tracing its performance or settling a billing question takes extra lookup that an identifiable name would have answered directly.

educate.base:
Programmatic deals and campaigns are the objects a publisher reconciles against an agreement and a buyer. In SpringServe, a private marketplace deal carries a deal ID that buyers and the exchange exchange in the bid request, and the publisher names the deal and the campaign objects on their side. The deal ID itself is a machine identifier defined by the OpenRTB specification, so it is not built for a human to read. The name the publisher attaches is what carries the meaning, and that name is what a report, an invoice, and a buyer conversation all rely on.

An identifiable name answers the questions a publisher asks when a deal comes up in a report. Which buyer is this. What were the terms. Which inventory does it cover. When the name encodes those facts consistently, a row in a deal report points straight to the agreement behind it, and a discrepancy between what SpringServe recorded and what the buyer reports can be settled by reading the name rather than chasing a spreadsheet. When deal and campaign names are generic, inconsistent, or built from internal shorthand that only one person understands, the same reconciliation turns into a lookup every time.

The risk grows as the number of deals grows. A handful of deals can be held in memory. A book of dozens cannot, and the name is the only durable label that travels with the deal through every report and export. Naming a deal after the buyer and the terms, in the same pattern every time, is the difference between a deal book a new team member can read and one that depends on the person who set it up.

A few signs point to deal and campaign names that are not identifiable:
  • Deal names do not encode the buyer, the terms, or the inventory, so a deal cannot be matched to its agreement from the name alone
  • Campaign names use internal shorthand or inconsistent patterns that only the person who created them can decode
  • Two deals with different buyers or terms carry names so similar that a report cannot tell them apart
You may want to confirm that deal and campaign names follow a consistent pattern that ties each object back to its buyer and terms. One approach is to define a deal-naming pattern that includes the buyer and a short term reference, then apply it to new deals and rename the existing ones that do not match.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster, programmatic deals often map to specific channels, dayparts, or content genres that a buyer paid to reach. You may want deal names to encode the buyer and the content or daypart the deal targets, so a report can show how each buyer's deal performed against the inventory it was struck for rather than a single blended deal line.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher, deals may be tied to a sponsoring brand or a product category whose video inventory the buyer wanted. You may want deal and campaign names to mark the sponsoring brand and the category, so a report keeps each sponsorship distinct and a billing question can be settled from the name without opening the underlying agreement.

educate.learn_more_note: SpringServe's detailed configuration documentation for deals and campaigns is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the deal and campaign names in the account to see whether each one ties back to its buyer and terms and whether the names follow a consistent pattern.

springserve_ui.label: SpringServe UI
steps: Open the deals list and read each deal name, noting whether it encodes the buyer, the terms, and the inventory the deal covers.
Open the campaign list and check whether campaign names follow a consistent pattern that ties each one back to its purpose and buyer.
Record any deal or campaign whose name is generic, built from internal shorthand, or so similar to another that the two cannot be told apart in a report.
Confirm whether a documented deal-naming pattern exists or whether names were set ad hoc as deals were added.

springserve_reporting.label: SpringServe reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal, then by campaign, and read the row labels as a buyer reconciliation would.
Note any deal that cannot be matched to its agreement from the name alone, and any pair of deals whose names are too similar to separate.
Record which buyers or terms the publisher would want a deal report to surface that the current names do not carry.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether deal and campaign names follow a consistent pattern that ties each object to its buyer and terms. A setup where names encode the buyer, the terms, and the inventory, and follow a documented pattern, passes. A setup where deal or campaign names are generic, built from inconsistent shorthand, or indistinguishable from one another is a fail recorded with the deals and campaigns affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether deal and campaign names tie back to their buyer and terms above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the deal and campaign names in {{site_name}}'s SpringServe account do not consistently identify the buyer, the terms, or the inventory each one covers, so a deal cannot always be matched to its agreement from the name alone. A private marketplace deal carries a machine deal ID defined by the OpenRTB specification, so the human-readable name the publisher attaches is what a report, an invoice, and a buyer conversation rely on. {{voice_recommend}} defining a deal-naming pattern that encodes the buyer and a short term reference, applying it to new deals, and renaming the existing deals and campaigns that do not match. After the rename, pull a report broken out by deal to confirm each row ties back to its buyer and terms.
